note.soap:
S: The patient is a 35-year-old adult presenting with 4 days of sinus pressure and congestion without fever or focal facial pain. There are no severe symptoms reported, and no orbital or focal facial findings. The patient denies severe red flags and has no chronic lung disease or medication allergies. Pregnancy status not captured.

O: No physical exam available due to phone-only encounter. No fever reported. No focal facial tenderness or orbital symptoms noted. Duration of symptoms is 4 days.

A:  
  - Working diagnosis (most likely): Acute viral sinusitis  
  - Differential: Bacterial sinusitis, allergic rhinitis, upper respiratory viral infection  
  - Red flags considered: Orbital cellulitis, severe facial pain, high fever, neurological symptoms—all denied or not present

P:  
  - Disposition: Manage conservatively with primary care follow-up if symptoms worsen or persist beyond 10 days. Urgent care if new severe symptoms develop.  
  - Plan: Recommend symptomatic treatment including nasal saline irrigation, oral analgesics such as acetaminophen or ibuprofen, and nasal decongestants for short-term use (no more than 3 days). Antibiotics are not indicated at this time given lack of bacterial infection signs.  
  - Safety net: Advise patient to seek urgent care if fever develops, facial swelling or severe pain occurs, vision changes, or if symptoms worsen or do not improve after 10 days.

Evidence (snippets used):  
- Duration: 4 days  
- Fever: no  
- Focal face: no  
- Severe: no  
- Orbital or focal: no  
- Severe red flags: false  
- Chronic lung: none  
- Med allergies: none
patient_instructions: If you are not improving as expected, or if symptoms worsen, seek in-person medical care.
Medication options you may consider if safe (no dosing by phone): acetaminophen (Tylenol) if you can take it safely; ibuprofen (Advil) if you can take it safely.
